title: Dolly Varden
content:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Dear Dolly! who does not recall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The thrilling page that pictured all
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Those charms that held our sense in thrall
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Just as the artist caught her,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ As down that English lane she tripped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In bowered chintz, hat sideways tipped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Trim-bodiced, bright-eyed, roguish-lipped,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The locksmithן¿½s pretty daughter?

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Sweet fragment of the Masterן¿½s art!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ O simple faith! O rustic heart!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ O maid that hath no counterpart
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In lifeן¿½s dry, dog-eared pages!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Where shall we find thy like? Ah, stay!
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Methinks I saw her yesterday
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ In chintz that flowered, as one might say,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Perennial for ages.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her fatherן¿½s modest cot was stone,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Five stories high; in style and tone
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Composite, and, I frankly own,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Within its walls revealing
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some certain novel, strange ideas:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Gothic door with Roman piers,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And floors removed some thousand years,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ From their Pompeian ceiling.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The small salon where she received
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Was Louis Quatorze, and relieved
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ By Chinese cabinets, conceived
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Grotesquely by the heathen;
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The sofas were a classic sight,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The Roman bench (sedilia hight);
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The chairs were French in gold and white,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And one Elizabethan.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And she, the goddess of that shrine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Two ringed fingers placed in mine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ The stones were many carats fine,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And of the purest water,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Then dropped a curtsy, far enough
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ To fairly fill her cretonne puff
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ And show the petticoatן¿½s rich stuff
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That her fond parent bought her.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her speech was simple as her dress,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Not French the more, but English less,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She loved; yet sometimes, I confess,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ I scarce could comprehend her.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Her manners were quite far from shy.
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ There was a quiet in her eye
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Appalling to the Hugh whoן¿½d try
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ With rudeness to offend her.

ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½But whence,ן¿½ I cried, ן¿½this masquerade?
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Some figure for to-nightן¿½s charade,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ A Watteau shepherdess or maid?ן¿½
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ She smiled and begged my pardon:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ ן¿½Why, surely you must know the name,
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ That woman who was Shakespeareן¿½s flame
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Or Byronן¿½s, well, itן¿½s all the same:
ֲ ֲ ֲ ֲ Why, Lord! Iן¿½m Dolly Varden!ן¿½
meaning:
"Dolly Varden" is a quintessential example of Bret Harte (Francis)'s signature style...

### Why We Love This Line
At Linespedia, we believe that poetry is the ultimate sanctuary for the soul...
